real_projects_tutorial.title: Why Building Real Projects Taught Me More Than Any Tutorial
real_projects_tutorial.excerpt: There's something exciting about starting a new coding tutorial. You follow along, everything works, the UI looks clean — and you feel like you're learning. But then you try building something from scratch...

content.intro_1: There's something exciting about starting a new coding tutorial. You follow along, everything works, the UI looks clean — and you feel like you're learning.
content.intro_2: But then you try building something from scratch.
content.intro_3: Suddenly, nothing is handed to you. You're the one deciding how things should work, where to fetch data, how to handle empty states, loading states, slow networks, form validation, weird bugs. You realize quickly: tutorials are helpful, but they don't prepare you for the real mess of real apps.
content.section_1_item_2: What to do when an API fails
content.section_1_item_3: What happens when your design breaks on mobile
content.section_1_item_4: How to talk through bugs and edge cases with someone else
content.section_1_item_5: How to keep code clean even as features grow
content.section_1_paragraph_after_list: All those things? I only started learning them when I stopped watching and started building.
content.section_2_paragraph: When you're building your own app, you're not just copying code — you're making decisions. That's where the real growth happens. You search, test, refactor, hit bugs, fix them, hit new ones, and repeat.
content.section_2_conclusion: You don't just learn "how to use React" — you learn how to think like a developer.
content.section_3_paragraph_1: Working on real apps at my job pushed this even further.
content.section_3_paragraph_2: Sometimes I ship something, feel good about it — then get feedback that it doesn't work quite right. Or that I missed tiny design details I hadn't even noticed. I go back, fix it, and still get more notes.
content.section_3_paragraph_3: That feedback loop — build, test, break, improve — is where I've learned the most. Not just about code, but about quality, precision, and thinking clearly.
content.section_3_paragraph_4: Sure, the feedback can be direct. Sometimes it stings. But I've learned to treat it like a debugging tool for my own thinking. It keeps me sharp and helps me level up way faster than finishing a course ever did.
content.section_4_paragraph_1: If you're serious about improving as a developer, you have to build.
content.section_4_paragraph_2: Not tutorials. Not clone apps with all the answers given. Real projects — where you don't know everything upfront, and you have to figure things out as you go.
content.section_4_paragraph_3: That's where the growth is. That's where the learning sticks.

ai_wont_replace.title: AI Won't Replace Developers - It Will Change How We Build
ai_wont_replace.excerpt: There's a lot of noise lately about AI replacing developers. I don't buy it. What AI is doing is speeding up development, sometimes a lot. But speed alone doesn't build good software.

content.section_1_paragraph_1: AI can generate code, suggest solutions, and help with repetitive work. That's useful. I use it. But it doesn't actually understand the product, the users, or the long-term consequences of a technical decision.
content.section_1_paragraph_2: In practice, AI often overcomplicates simple problems. It introduces abstractions you don't need, solves the wrong thing confidently, or misses issues that are obvious once you actually understand how the codebase works. Spotting those problems still requires a developer who knows what they're doing.
content.section_2_paragraph_1: AI needs developers who understand code, not the other way around.
content.section_2_paragraph_2: Used well, AI is a productivity boost. Used blindly, it creates fragile systems that are hard to maintain. The difference isn't the tool it's the person using it.
content.section_3_paragraph_1: I think the real skill going forward isn't competing with AI, but learning how to control it. Knowing when to accept a suggestion, when to simplify it, and when to throw it away completely.
content.section_3_paragraph_2: AI should speed us up, not replace our thinking.
content.section_3_paragraph_3: We still need to understand the code we ship and take responsibility for it.

building_a_streaming_chatbot.title: Building a Streaming Chatbot with Vercel AI SDK: What I Learned
building_a_streaming_chatbot.excerpt: I wanted my website to be more than a static page. Instead of just listing projects and links, I wanted visitors to be able to ask questions and get immediate answers, something that feels closer to a real conversation.

content.intro_1: I wanted my website to be more than a static page. Instead of just listing projects and links, I wanted visitors to be able to ask questions and get immediate answers, something that feels closer to a real conversation.
content.intro_2: At first, I considered building a chatbot from scratch. But once you get into it, things get complicated quickly: HTTP streaming, managing partial responses, message state, loading indicators, and error handling. That's a lot of infrastructure for a portfolio project.
content.intro_3: So I used Vercel AI SDK.
content.section_1_paragraph_1: Without streaming, chatbots feel slow and awkward. You send a message, wait a couple of seconds, and then the full response appears all at once. Even if the response is fast, the experience feels broken.
content.section_1_paragraph_2: With streaming, users see the response appear as it's being generated. The first words show up almost immediately, which makes the interaction feel responsive and natural. Even if the full response takes a second or two, users feel engaged instead of waiting.
content.section_2_title: What the SDK Gave Me (and What It Didn't)
content.section_2_paragraph_1: The biggest win with Vercel AI SDK is how much complexity it removes:
content.section_2_item_1: Streaming responses without manually handling HTTP streams
content.section_2_item_2: Message and loading state handled via useChat
content.section_2_paragraph_2: That said, it's not "magic." You still have to think about:
content.section_2_item_6: Rate limiting (I capped it at 10 chats per IP per day)
content.section_2_item_7: Context size (long conversations get expensive and slow)
content.section_2_paragraph_3: The SDK makes the hard parts manageable, but the architectural decisions are still yours.
content.section_3_paragraph_1: I wouldn't use a streaming chatbot for everything:
content.section_3_item_1: If the content is static, a FAQ is simpler and cheaper
content.section_3_item_2: For high-traffic sites, API costs add up quickly
content.section_3_item_3: For sensitive data, sending prompts to third-party LLMs isn't acceptable
content.section_3_paragraph_2: But for a portfolio with moderate traffic? It's a great fit.
content.section_4_paragraph_1: Using Vercel AI SDK let me focus on the experience instead of building streaming infrastructure from scratch. It turned what could've been weeks of work into something approachable, while still forcing me to think about real-world trade-offs.
content.section_4_paragraph_2: For me, it wasn't about adding "AI" to a portfolio, it was about improving how people interact with it. And in that context, streaming made all the difference.
